blocks.text: The New Deal
by Dan Fakkeldy
Canada Post, CUPW, and What It Means for Rural Mail
Chapter 1 - The Post Office on Main Street

The post office in Port Hood sits at 218 Main Street, a white-sided Canada Post corporate office between the fire hall and the credit union. It opens at half past eight on a weekday morning. By nine, the parking lot has a handful of cars, and the first rural carrier has already loaded up and headed west along the shore road toward Mabou. Inside, there is the familiar counter, the postal boxes for people who rent them, the shelf where parcels wait to be picked up. On a Thursday, the door stays open until six. Every other weekday, it closes at five. Weekends, it is shut.

This is the post office that serves a community of about four hundred people on the western coast of Cape Breton, in Inverness County, Nova Scotia. Port Hood is the county seat, such as it is — the place where people come for the library, the beach, the ceilidh trail visitors centre in summer, and the mail. The post office anchors the village the way it anchors hundreds of small Canadian communities: not as a shop you visit by choice, but as a service point you depend on almost without thinking about it. Government cheques arrive here. Online purchases. Christmas cards from Alberta. Election ballots. The machinery of a town runs through this counter.

Think about what flows through a rural post office in a week. On a Monday, the Canada Child Benefit cheques for families in the area arrive, and people come in to pick them up or find them in their box. On a Wednesday, a wave of parcels from online orders — Amazon, Well.ca, a birthday gift from a grandchild in Toronto — fills the shelf behind the counter, each one waiting for the small yellow card in your mailbox that tells you something is waiting. On a Friday, the local flyer bundle goes out — the grocery store specials, the hardware store sale, the municipal notice about road work. For someone running a small business from their home — and in rural Cape Breton, many people do — the post office is how they ship what they sell and receive what they need. For someone without reliable internet — and that is still a reality in parts of rural Nova Scotia — the post office is how they interact with the government, the bank, the pharmacy. It is easy, in a city with a post office on every block, to think of postal service as one utility among many. In a place like Port Hood, it is closer to infrastructure.

But the work of actually carrying that mail — of driving it out to the rural addresses along the coast and inland toward Judique and Troy — that does not happen at the counter. That happens in a different world of Canada Post, one with its own history, its own pay structure, and its own labour contract. And in June of 2026, that world got a new five-year deal that changes some of its most basic rules.

To understand what that deal means — for the people who carry mail out of this post office, and for the people who depend on that mail arriving — you need to understand something that most Canadians never think about: Canada Post does not run on one set of rules. It runs on two.

The first world is Urban Postal Operations. That is the formal name, and it covers what you probably picture when you think of a letter carrier: someone in a Canada Post jacket walking a city route with a satchel, sorting mail in a plant overnight, working out of a big depot in Halifax or Dartmouth or Toronto. Urban carriers are paid an hourly wage. They have set schedules. Their routes are measured in points of call — the addresses they walk to — and their day is structured around completing those points in a set number of hours. When people talk about postal workers on the news, they are almost always talking about the Urban unit. About fifty-five thousand CUPW members work in Urban Postal Operations.

The second world is the one that matters here. It is called the Rural and Suburban Mail Carriers unit, usually shortened to RSMC. These are the carriers who drive. They cover the rural routes, the suburban loops, the long stretches of highway between scattered addresses. An RSMC route out of Port Hood might run from the village itself out along the shore, looping through Mabou and back through the inland roads, covering a hundred kilometres or more in a morning. The carrier drives their own vehicle, or a Canada Post vehicle if one is assigned. They stop at community mailboxes, at roadside mailboxes, at the cluster boxes in subdivisions. They carry parcels as well as letters. Their work is measured not by hours walked but by the route itself — its length, its volume, its stops.

There are about eight thousand RSMCs across Canada. Seventy percent of them are women. That detail matters because it tells you something about who this job has historically been: a route you could take on while raising a family, often taken on by women in rural communities who needed income that could be earned from a car and a set of boxes. Roughly half of RSMCs work out of offices that also have Urban letter carriers and postal clerks. The other half work out of smaller rural post offices, often alongside rural postmasters who belong to a different union entirely — the Canadian Postmasters and Assistants Association, or CPAA. Port Hood is the kind of office where the two worlds meet: a corporate post office with counter staff and, radiating out from it, the rural routes served by RSMCs.

The two units have separate collective agreements. Separate wage tables. Separate classification systems. Separate histories. When CUPW and Canada Post sit down to bargain, they negotiate two contracts at once: one for Urban, one for RSMC. The two contracts often move in parallel — the same wage increase percentage, the same pension language, the same benefit improvements — but the fine print diverges, because the work diverges. An Urban carrier and an RSMC could work for the same employer, under the same union, out of the same building, and have radically different rules governing how they are paid, how their work is measured, and what happens when they are absent or their route changes.

For many years, RSMCs were not even considered employees. They were contractors — independent operators who bid on routes and got paid a flat rate per route, with no benefits, no pension, no job security. That was the system until CUPW organized them and won a first collective agreement. The contractor history is the reason the route-evaluation pay system exists at all: it grew out of a world where a carrier was paid for the route as a unit of work, not for the time spent doing it. That system is now being dismantled. Understanding why — and what replaces it — is one of the central stories of the new contract.

The contractor-to-employee transition is worth understanding in a bit more detail, because it explains why the RSMC world looks the way it does. Before CUPW representation, a rural mail carrier was essentially a small business owner. They held a contract to deliver mail on a route. They provided their own vehicle, paid for their own gas, covered their own maintenance, and bore their own insurance. They were paid a flat amount per route, negotiated periodically with Canada Post. If the route got longer — more houses built along the road, a new subdivision added — the carrier had to negotiate a new rate, and the negotiation favored the party with more information and more leverage, which was Canada Post. There were no benefits. No vacation pay. No sick leave. No pension. If you could not work — because of illness, injury, or family obligations — you found someone to cover your route, and you paid them out of your own pocket. If you could not find someone, the mail did not go out.

CUPW's organizing campaign among RSMCs was one of the most significant labor victories in Canada in the early 2000s. It brought a predominantly female, rural, historically marginalized workforce into a collective bargaining framework. The first collective agreement, achieved in 2003, converted RSMCs from contractors to employees, gave them benefits, gave them a pension, gave them contractual rights. But the pay system — the route evaluation — remained, because it was the structure the parties inherited, and replacing it was a bigger fight than winning the first contract. The route-evaluation model has persisted through every subsequent round of bargaining. Until now.

So here is the question this book will work through. On June eighteenth, 2026, Canada Post and CUPW signed new collective agreements for both the Urban and RSMC bargaining units, covering the period through January thirty-first, 2029. The RSMC members voted eighty-five point nine percent in favour. The Urban members voted eighty-nine percent in favour. The agreements include wage increases, pension protections, benefit improvements, personal days, a new weekend parcel delivery model, and, for RSMCs specifically, the most significant structural change to their pay and classification system in a generation. Those are the headlines. But headlines, in labour contracts, are where the interesting questions begin — not where they end.

What does it actually mean when an RSMC moves from a route-evaluation pay system to an hourly rate? Who is an OCRE — an On-Call Relief Employee — and why does this new contract change the rules around OCREs in ways that could reshape how rural routes get covered? What happens when a route holder in Port Hood takes a vacation under the new absence-coverage rules? Why does Canada Post need a weekend parcel model, and what does that have to do with a two-hundred-million-dollar quarterly loss and two billion dollars in federal loans? And underneath all of it: is this a good deal for the people who carry your mail, or a restructuring dressed up as a raise?

The honest answer, which this book will build toward, is that it is both. And the part that matters most — the part that will determine what rural mail looks like in Cape Breton and across Canada five years from now — lives not in the wage numbers but in the structural fine print. The route measurement formulas. The new classifications. The coverage hierarchy. The clauses that govern what happens when a route is recalculated, or when a post office is reviewed, or when a government decides that maintaining the status quo is not an option.

We will get to all of it. But first, we need to understand how we got here — how two years of chaos, a strike, a government intervention, and a commission of inquiry produced the deal that eight-five-point-nine percent of rural carriers voted to accept.
Chapter 2 - How We Got Here

The round of bargaining that produced the 2026 collective agreements began in November of 2023. That is worth pausing on, because it means the contract covering postal workers through January of 2029 started as a conversation more than two years before the ink dried. Two years of proposals and counter-proposals, a national strike, a government order sending workers back to their routes, a federally appointed commission of inquiry, and finally a tentative agreement that took another six months to ratify. To understand why the deal looks the way it does — why CUPW accepted terms far below its opening demands, why Canada Post pushed for structural changes it had wanted for years, why the federal government was leaning on both parties the whole time — you need to understand the sequence.

Canada Post and CUPW had been operating without new contracts since the previous agreements expired. The 2023 round was always going to be difficult because the two sides were not just arguing about money. They were arguing about what Canada Post is for, and what kind of workforce it should have. Canada Post, backed by the federal government, wanted what it called transformation: a more flexible workforce, new delivery models, weekend parcel operations, and changes to how rural carriers are paid and how their routes are structured. CUPW wanted wage increases that reflected inflation, protections against contracting out, improvements to the route-evaluation system — or better, its replacement with an hourly rate — and stronger job security, especially for the most precarious workers.

Through 2024, the gap between those positions was enormous. Canada Post presented what it called global offers — comprehensive proposals bundling wages, benefits, and structural changes together. CUPW rejected them and presented its own counter-offers. By November of 2024, CUPW's RSMC negotiating committee had put forward a second global offer that sought wage increases of nine percent in the first year, five percent in the second, four percent in the third, and four percent in the fourth — twenty-two percent over four years, or nearly twenty-four percent compounded. That offer also demanded an hourly rate system with proper time values, corporate vehicles for all RSMCs, a guaranteed forty-hour week, improved disability and injury pay, ten paid medical days, and, notably, improved rights for On-Call Relief Employees. It was an ambitious package, and it reflected what the union believed rural carriers were owed after years of being treated as a cheaper, more flexible alternative to Urban workers.

Canada Post's offers were nowhere close. The corporation wanted a seven-year wage progression instead of five, meaning new hires would take longer to reach the top pay rate. It wanted two-tier structures for pensions and benefits. It wanted the flexibility to create part-time classifications that would support weekend delivery without paying overtime to full-time workers. The distance between these positions was not a disagreement about percentages. It was a disagreement about the future shape of the workforce.

That is the background against which the strike happened. In November of 2024, fifty-five thousand CUPW members walked out. The strike lasted four weeks. Mail and parcels stopped moving across the country during the busiest shipping season of the year. Businesses scrambled to find alternatives. Online retailers warned customers about delays. And then the federal government — the Trudeau Liberal government, still in power at that point — intervened. Using a provision called Section 107 of the Canada Labour Code, the government ordered the Canada Industrial Relations Board to supervise a vote on Canada Post's latest offer, effectively forcing workers back to their routes. The legal mechanism was a reinterpretation of a section that had been used before in labour disputes, but its application here was controversial. CUPW called it an abuse of the law to break a lawful strike. The government said it was necessary to protect the economy during the holiday season. Either way, the strike was over. Workers were back on the job by Christmas, but without a new contract.

The strike did not produce an agreement. It produced a stalemate, and the stalemate produced something else: an Industrial Inquiry Commission. This is a tool the federal government has for intractable labour disputes. It appoints a commissioner — in this case, a lawyer named William Kaplan — to investigate the conflict, hear from both sides, and produce a report with recommendations. The commission began hearings in early 2025, and its report came out at the end of May.

The hearings themselves were a significant event in the labour world. The commissioner heard from Canada Post executives, who presented the financial picture — declining letter mail, parcel competition, mounting losses — and argued that the corporation needed a fundamentally different workforce model to survive. He heard from CUPW leadership and rank-and-file members, who testified about precarious classifications, the route-evaluation system's failures, the physical toll of the work, and the erosion of living standards. He heard from customers, from small businesses, from municipal leaders, from academics who study Crown corporations and public services. The hearings were, in effect, a public examination of what Canada Post is for and who it should serve — a question that had been deferred for years through the negotiation rounds but could no longer be avoided.

The Industrial Inquiry Commission report was the pivot. It did not impose a settlement, but it shaped the framework within which the rest of the negotiation happened. The report acknowledged that Canada Post faced a genuine financial crisis and that the status quo was unsustainable. It also acknowledged that workers had legitimate grievances about precarious classifications, disability pay, and the route-evaluation system. Crucially, the commission reported that the parties had actually reached some tentative agreements on specific issues during its facilitated mediation — including on the RSMC Permanent Flex Employee conversion and on improvements to the Short-Term Disability Program. The commission recommended that the final collective agreements should reflect and build on those mediated tentative agreements. That recommendation mattered because it gave both sides political cover to accept things they had already partially agreed to, while framing the remaining gaps as problems to be solved rather than battles to be won.

What the report also did was make clear that the federal government was going to be involved in Canada Post's future whether the union liked it or not. The government was preparing to provide financial support to keep the corporation solvent, and it was preparing to attach conditions to that support. Those conditions would become public in September of 2025, when the government authorized a sweeping restructuring plan: eliminating door-to-door delivery for the roughly four million Canadian addresses that still had it, expanding community mailboxes, lengthening delivery standards, and reviewing the post office network. This was not a CUPW demand or a Canada Post bargaining position — it was a government mandate that would reshape the environment in which any collective agreement had to operate.

Negotiations continued through the summer and fall of 2025, now under the shadow of the commission's report and the looming restructuring plan. The tone shifted. Both sides had spent two years demonstrating their willingness to fight — the union through the strike, the corporation through its global offers and its public messaging about financial unsustainability. Now the path to a deal ran through the commission's framework: accept the mediated tentative agreements, find compromises on the remaining structural issues, and produce something that members could ratify.

On November twenty-first, 2025, the parties announced that they had reached agreements in principle. They agreed to suspend all strike and lockout activity while the contractual language was finalized. A month later, on December twenty-second, they announced full tentative agreements for both the Urban and RSMC bargaining units. Summaries were published. The key terms were out.

But this was not the end of the process — it was the start of the ratification phase, and ratification took another six months. Through January of 2026, the parties worked to finalize outstanding contractual language that the December summaries had not fully resolved. The full draft collective agreements were released to members on February twenty-fourth. CUPW then announced the voting period: April twentieth through May thirtieth. Members would vote in person or online, and they would simultaneously vote on a separate ballot authorizing strike action, in case ratification failed and the conflict resumed.

The voting period itself was long — six weeks, an acknowledgment of how much was at stake and how much members needed to absorb. CUPW published a series of detailed bulletins explaining specific aspects of the RSMC tentative agreement: what was changing for OCREs, how absence coverage would work, the new wage progression table, improvements to disability and personal days. These bulletins were where the structural details — the OCRE group expansion, the removal of the seventy-five-kilometre vacancy restriction, the new coverage hierarchy — became clear to members.

The vote came on June first, 2026. Eighty-nine percent of Urban members voted yes. Eighty-five point nine percent of RSMC members voted yes. The margins were strong but not unanimous, and within CUPW's own national executive, five of fifteen members had voted against the deal. The agreement had critics inside the union as well as outside it. Some members saw it as the best achievable result under the circumstances — a government-backed employer facing a financial crisis, a commission report that favoured compromise, and a restructuring mandate that would reshape Canada Post regardless of what the contract said. Others saw it as a capitulation, a deal that traded structural concessions for wage increases while leaving the most precarious workers exposed. Both of those views have merit, and this book will not resolve which one is more correct. It will, instead, give you the information to hold them both honestly.

Seventeen days after the vote, on June eighteenth, Canada Post and CUPW signed the new collective agreements. They are in effect until January thirty-first, 2029. The bargaining round that started in November of 2023 was over. The work of implementing what was agreed — and living with what was conceded — had just begun.

To see what that means in practice, we need to open up the agreement itself and walk through what it actually says. We will start with the shared terms, the ones both bargaining units got, because those are the headlines most people have heard. And then, chapter by chapter, we will move into the RSMC-specific changes — the ones that will reshape the daily reality of rural mail delivery in places like Port Hood.
Chapter 3 - The Headlines

Let us start with the numbers, because they are what most people heard about first. The new collective agreements — both the Urban and RSMC versions — include wage increases structured over the five-year term. In the first year, covering 2024, the wage increase is six and a half percent. In the second year, 2025, it is three percent. In the third year, 2026, it is two percent. For the fourth and fifth years, 2027 and 2028, the annual wage increase matches the Consumer Price Index — the official inflation rate.

That six-and-a-half-percent figure for year one deserves a closer look, because it is not quite what it sounds like. Five percent of that increase was already paid out in December of 2024, during the period when the parties were negotiating and the strike had just ended. So the additional wage increase retroactively owed for 2024 is one and a half percent — the remainder of the six and a half. That does not make the headline false, but it does mean that if you are a postal worker looking at your pay stub, the money you actually feel in year one is smaller than the number on the bargaining summary. Everyone covered by the agreement is owed retroactive pay for the 2024, 2025, and 2026 increases, because the contract was signed in June of 2026 but the increases are dated back to the start of each year. How that retroactive pay is calculated and when it arrives is being worked out between the parties. The principle is straightforward: you get the money, it just comes later.

The later years of the wage structure are where a genuine philosophical choice was made. Years one through three have fixed percentages: six and a half, three, and two. Years four and five are tied to the Consumer Price Index, the government's measure of inflation. This means that if inflation runs at, say, three percent in 2027, the wage increase that year is three percent. If inflation is one percent, the increase is one percent. The union had argued for a Cost of Living Adjustment, often called a COLA — a mechanism that would automatically boost wages to keep pace with rising prices, above and beyond the negotiated increase. That demand did not make it into the final agreement in the form CUPW wanted. Instead, the CPI-tied years function as a kind of inflation matching, but they are not an additional cost-of-living layer on top of a guaranteed raise. They are the raise itself. This is one of the points critics of the deal raise: that tying wages to CPI while not including a robust COLA mechanism means that if inflation outpaces expectations in 2027 and 2028, real wages — what the money actually buys — could erode. Defenders of the deal point out that CPI matching is better than a fixed low percentage, and that the earlier years' increases outpaced recent inflation. Both arguments are honestly made.

Now, the pension. This is where the agreement gave ground to the union's position, and it matters more than the wage numbers suggest. Canada Post workers in both bargaining units have a Defined Benefit pension plan. A Defined Benefit plan — DB plan for short — is the kind where your pension in retirement is calculated based on a formula, usually involving your years of service and your salary, rather than depending on how well investment markets performed. It is the more secure, more expensive kind of pension, and it has been disappearing from the private sector for decades. Canada Post, in its earlier global offers, had proposed two-tier pension changes: keeping the DB plan for existing workers but moving new hires to a different, less generous structure. CUPW fought that proposal hard, and in the final agreement, there are no changes to the Defined Benefit pension. Existing workers keep it. New hires get it too. That is a significant win, because pension structure is the kind of thing that compounds over decades, and a two-tier pension system would have meant a permanently divided workforce — one group with secure retirement, one without.

The benefits improvements in the agreement are real but less dramatic. The health benefits plan — which covers things like prescription drugs, dental, and vision for employees and their families — is enhanced. The income replacement for Injury on Duty leave and for Short-Term Disability Program leave is improved. Injury on Duty, often called IOD, is the leave you use when you are hurt at work — a significant issue for carriers, whose jobs involve driving, lifting, walking on ice, and all the physical risks of working outside in Canadian weather. Picture a rural carrier in February, getting out of the vehicle fifty times a morning, walking across icy driveways to cluster boxes, lifting parcels that weigh thirty pounds, navigating unplowed rural roads. The injury rate is real, and the IOD system is what stands between an injured carrier and financial hardship. The Short-Term Disability Program, or STDP, covers non-work-related illness and injury. Both programs had been criticized by CUPW for providing insufficient income replacement, especially for lower-paid and precarious workers. The improvements in the new agreement increase the percentage of your salary that gets replaced while you are on these leaves, reducing the financial penalty for getting sick or hurt.

Then there are the personal days. The agreement locks in six new non-carry-over personal days, bringing the total number of personal days in the collective agreement to thirteen. Non-carry-over means you use them in the year they are granted or you lose them. Personal days are distinct from vacation days and sick leave — they are the days you can take for reasons that do not fit neatly into other categories: a family obligation, a mental health day, a bureaucratic errand that can only be done on a Tuesday. Six additional personal days, guaranteed in the contract rather than left to management discretion, is a concrete quality-of-life improvement. It sounds small, but for shift workers and route holders whose schedules are rigid, the difference between a discretionary day and a contractual one is the difference between asking and telling.

These shared terms — wages, pension, benefits, personal days — are the parts of the agreement that apply to both bargaining units equally. If you stopped reading here, you would have a reasonably positive picture: wage increases, a protected pension, better disability coverage, more personal days. And for Urban workers, that picture is more or less complete, because the Urban agreement, while it includes the new weekend parcel delivery model and some classification changes, does not fundamentally restructure how Urban carriers are paid or how their work is measured.

For RSMCs, the shared terms are the prologue. The main event — the structural transformation that will reshape rural mail delivery — is what comes next. And it starts with the biggest change of all: the end of the route-evaluation pay system and the move to an hourly rate.

Before we go there, it is worth pausing on what the shared headlines tell us about the bargaining dynamic. CUPW's national president, Jan Simpson, framed the deal as a hard-won result under difficult circumstances. The union highlighted the pension protection, the wage increases, the benefit improvements, and the new RSMC provisions as evidence that collective bargaining had delivered real gains despite the financial pressure and government involvement. Canada Post framed the deal as a necessary step toward transformation, saying the agreements recognized that the corporation needed to change and were critical to returning to financial sustainability. The federal government stayed mostly quiet publicly, but its hand was visible throughout: the bailout money, the restructuring mandate, the commission report that shaped the framework.

And the critics — both inside and outside the union — pointed to what was not in the headlines. The cost-of-living protections that were suspended rather than strengthened. The new classifications that would create a more flexible, more precarious workforce. The workload measurement systems that would give management new tools to recalculate routes. The fact that while wages went up in percentage terms, the structural changes to how work is measured and compensated could mean that some carriers earn less in practice, even with a higher hourly rate, because the hours themselves get recalculated.

These are not conspiracy theories. They are the honest tension built into the agreement's structure. The wage increase is real. The structural changes are also real. Whether the first offsets the second, for any individual carrier, will depend on details that will play out over the coming years — details like how the new workload measurement formulas get applied, how aggressively route verification is used, and whether the pay protection during the transition holds up as routes are recalculated.

That is why the next chapters matter. The headlines tell you what the agreement promises. The structural changes tell you what it will actually do. And for RSMCs, the biggest structural change is the one that rewrites the most basic fact of their working life: how they get paid.
Chapter 4 - The RSMC Revolution

Of everything in the new collective agreement, the change that will most reshape the daily life of a rural carrier is the one that sounds the most technical: RSMCs are moving from a route-evaluation pay system to an hourly rate. That sentence, stripped of its jargon, means the fundamental unit of compensation is changing. Under the old system, an RSMC was paid for the route. Under the new system, an RSMC will be paid for the hours. It sounds simple. It is not. Understanding what this change means — who fought for it, what it replaces, and what it introduces — is the key to understanding whether the new deal is a victory or a restructuring for the people who carry rural mail.

Let us start with what the old system was, because you cannot evaluate the replacement without understanding what it replaced. The route-evaluation model grew directly out of RSMCs' history as contractors. For decades, rural mail delivery in Canada was done by independent operators — people who held a contract to deliver mail on a specific route and were paid a flat amount for doing so. The contract paid for the route as a unit of work, regardless of how many hours it took. If you were efficient and finished early, you were paid the same. If you were careful and methodical and it took longer, you were paid the same. There was no overtime, because there were no hours — only the route.

When CUPW organized RSMCs and won a first collective agreement, the contractor legacy persisted in the pay structure. Instead of an hourly wage like Urban carriers had, RSMCs were paid based on an evaluation of their route. The route was assessed — its length, its number of stops, the volume of mail and parcels it typically carried, the type of terrain, whether it was on foot or by vehicle — and that assessment produced a structured workload value. That value translated into a dollar amount. The carrier was paid that amount for completing the route, period. The system was called various things over the years — the Mail Delivery Measurement System, route evaluation, the structured workload — but the principle was constant: the route, not the clock, was the basis of pay.

CUPW opposed this system for as long as it existed, and for good reasons that had nothing to do with ideology. The route-evaluation system meant that if the volume of mail on your route increased — more parcels, more flyers, more households — your pay did not necessarily increase to match, because the evaluation had been done at some earlier point and might not reflect current reality. You could end up doing significantly more work for the same pay. It also meant that there was no clean concept of overtime. If the evaluated work for your route was supposed to take seven hours and it actually took nine, because of weather, or construction, or a surge in parcel volume, you were not automatically paid for those extra two hours at a premium rate. The system was opaque, difficult to challenge, and structurally disposed to pay carriers for less than they actually did.

There is another reason the route-evaluation system was contentious, and it has to do with the demographics of the RSMC workforce. Seventy percent of RSMCs are women. The route-evaluation system, with its emphasis on route-as-unit and its flexibility about when and how the work got done, was often described as family-friendly — you could structure your day around childcare, school pickups, and other obligations. But that same flexibility masked the fact that carriers were frequently working unpaid hours. A route evaluation that said the work was seven hours might actually take nine on a routine day and eleven during the holiday parcel rush, and the carrier absorbed that difference. The family-friendly framing and the unpaid-labour reality were two sides of the same coin.

So when CUPW demanded an hourly rate for RSMCs, it was demanding something that sounded like basic fairness: pay people for the hours they work, the same way Urban carriers are paid. This was a long-standing demand, raised in round after round of bargaining. And in the new agreement, CUPW got it. RSMCs will move to an hourly rate of pay. By the union's framing, this is a generational achievement — the dismantling of a pay system that treated rural carriers as a category apart from their Urban colleagues.

But the hourly rate is not arriving on its own. It is arriving inside a new system for measuring and managing RSMC work, and that system is where the concerns live. The new framework has two main components with names that sound bureaucratic but whose mechanics matter enormously: the Work Content Model and the RSMC Workload Measurement System.

The Work Content Model is, in essence, a new way of defining what constitutes a full day of work for an RSMC. Instead of evaluating the route as a static unit and paying for it, the Work Content Model breaks the work into components — driving, delivering to mailboxes, delivering to doors, sorting, loading, parcel handling — and assigns time values to each component. The RSMC Workload Measurement System is the apparatus for applying those values: statistical sampling methods and measurement formulas that allow management to calculate how long a given route should take, based on its characteristics and the standardized time values. Together, these systems give management a centralized, formula-driven way to determine route structure, workload, and ultimately pay.

This is the tension at the heart of the change, and it is worth stating plainly. The hourly rate — the thing CUPW wanted — is being delivered through a workload measurement system that gives management significant new authority to define what counts as a full day's work. If the measurement system determines that a route that used to be evaluated at eight hours can actually be done in seven, then the route gets recalculated, and the carrier's hours — and therefore pay — are adjusted. The carrier who was efficient and finished the eight-hour route in seven hours under the old system now finds that the route is officially a seven-hour route, and they are paid for seven hours, not eight.

This is not hypothetical. The agreement includes language about what happens when a route holder's workload exceeds their structured assessment. If a route holder works five percent above their structured workload over a period of four consecutive weeks — December excluded, because December is always heavier — they can submit a request for route verification. Route verification is the process by which the measurement system is applied to check whether the route's assessed workload is still accurate. If the assessment is off, the route gets recalculated.

There is also a broader trigger: a route bid — a competitive process where carriers can bid on routes — takes place when, after an annual assessment, any route in the office is adjusted by ten percent, up or down. A ten-percent adjustment is a significant recalibration. It means the route has changed enough in volume, stops, or character that the carrier may want to rebid, or that the route needs to be restructured. The fact that route bids are triggered by these assessments means that the measurement system is not just a background administrative tool. It actively shapes the landscape of routes in an office, and therefore the landscape of jobs.

The new wage progression table compounds this. Under the previous system, RSMCs reached the maximum pay rate at Year 5 of their career. Under the new agreement, the starting rate for a new hire is eighty percent of the maximum rate, and employees reach the top rate at Year 7. The progression from starting to maximum pay now takes seven years instead of five. The agreement includes a protection clause: no current employee will see a decrease in their progression level or take longer to reach the maximum rate than they would have under the old system. This means that if you are already, say, at Year 4 when the new agreement takes effect, you will not be pushed back to an earlier progression step. But new hires — the people who will be entering the workforce over the next five years — will face a longer climb to the top rate. A two-year extension of the wage progression is a structural cost saving for the employer, because every new hire spends two more years at a lower pay rate. It is also the kind of change that does not affect anyone voting on the agreement today, which is why it is politically easier to accept.

Then there is the pay protection language during the transition. When an RSMC's route is recalculated under the new workload measurement system, the resulting change in evaluated hours — and therefore in pay — could go either way. Some routes might be assessed as heavier than previously evaluated, resulting in more hours and more pay. Others might be assessed as lighter, resulting in fewer hours and less pay. The agreement includes provisions to protect carriers during the initial transition: if a recalculation reduces a carrier's evaluated hours, there is a mechanism to cushion that reduction for a period. But the inclusion of pay protection language is itself an acknowledgment that recalculations are expected to reduce evaluated hours and earnings for significant numbers of carriers. You do not include pay protection if you expect recalculations to go up.

There is also a document associated with the implementation called the People Plan, which operates as a memorandum of understanding between the parties. The People Plan includes a limited no-layoff commitment, but its scope is narrow. The commitment applies to current regular employees — permanent route holders and permanent flex employees — and only to the initial restructuring of offices under the new workload measurement regime. It does not cover precarious classifications like On-Call Relief Employees. And it does not extend to subsequent reorganizations, route consolidations, or future restructuring driven by the broader transformation plan. The no-layoff commitment is real, but it is bounded. It buys stability for current permanent workers during the transition, and leaves the future open.

The overtime rules also deserve scrutiny, because they reveal how the two bargaining units remain different even when the language sounds similar. CUPW has said that under the new agreement, overtime rules for RSMCs will be the same as for the Urban unit. In practice, the thresholds differ in ways that disadvantage rural carriers. Urban carriers receive double-time — twice their hourly rate — after two hours of overtime in a day. RSMCs qualify for the higher overtime premium only after three hours of overtime. For an RSMC on a scheduled nine-hour shift, that means double-time does not kick in until the twelve-hour mark. The agreement also states that work beyond scheduled hours should be assigned in a manner to maximize work paid at straight time — meaning the regular hourly rate, not a premium. In plain English, management is instructed to structure additional work to avoid triggering overtime premiums where possible.

Now, step back and hold the two pictures side by side. CUPW's picture: after decades of fighting the route-evaluation system, rural carriers will finally be paid for their time, like every other worker. The opaque, contractor-legacy pay structure is gone. Carriers can request route verification when they are overworked. There are protections during the transition. This is progress, hard-won.

The critical picture: the hourly rate arrives wrapped in a workload measurement system that gives management centralized authority to define what a full day's work is, recalculate routes, and reorganize the workforce. The wage progression is extended by two years for new hires. Overtime thresholds are worse than Urban. The no-layoff commitment is narrow and temporary. Pay protection language exists because reductions are expected. The new system is not just a fairer way to pay people — it is also the administrative architecture for the route consolidation, workforce reduction, and efficiency targets that the government's transformation plan demands.

Both of these pictures are accurate. They are not competing explanations of the same fact. They are two true things about a complex change, and holding them together — without collapsing into either celebration or condemnation — is the only honest way to understand what the new pay system means for the people who will work under it.

What it means in a specific place — a rural office, a set of routes along the Cape Breton coast — depends on details that will unfold over years: which routes get recalculated and by how much, how aggressively the measurement system is applied, whether the pay protection holds, and whether the routes themselves survive the broader restructuring. We will come back to that. But first, we need to understand the other structural change that will reshape RSMC work life: the overhaul of how routes are staffed when the regular carrier is absent, and the new classifications of workers who do that staffing.
Chapter 5 - Who Covers the Route?

Every rural route has a regular carrier. That person — the route holder — is assigned to the route, knows its quirks, drives it daily, and is the face of the postal service on that stretch of road. But route holders get sick. They take vacations. They go on injury leave. They retire. And when they are not there, the mail still has to go out. In a city depot, covering an absent letter carrier is a routine administrative task: another carrier takes the route, or a relief carrier from a pool picks it up. In a rural office, covering an absent route is a more complex problem, because the routes are longer, the distances between them are greater, and the pool of available relief workers is smaller. How that problem gets solved — who gets called, in what order, from how far away, and under what rules — is the subject of the most detailed RSMC-specific changes in the new agreement. And at the centre of those changes is a classification of worker whose name most Canadians have never heard: the OCRE.

OCRE stands for On-Call Relief Employee. An OCRE is an RSMC who does not have a permanent route assignment. Instead, they are available to be called — on call, as the name says — to cover routes when the regular carrier is absent or when a route is vacant. The OCRE is the most flexible, most precarious classification in the RSMC bargaining unit. They have no guaranteed hours. They have no assigned route. They do not know from week to week whether they will be working, or where, or for how many hours. They are paid for the work they do when they are called, and they are not paid when they are not called. For someone trying to hold together a life — a family, a vehicle payment, a plan — that uncertainty is the defining feature of the job.

To understand why OCREs matter so much in the new agreement, you need to see them in context. The RSMC bargaining unit has, broadly speaking, four classifications of worker, and the new agreement changes the rules for all of them. Walking through each one shows you the staffing puzzle from the inside.

The first classification is the route holder. This is the permanent employee assigned to a specific rural route. They are the regular carrier. Under the old system, they were paid based on the route evaluation; under the new system, they will be paid an hourly rate based on the recalculated workload. They have the most stability — a known route, a known schedule, a known income. When people think of their rural mail carrier, they are usually thinking of a route holder.

The second classification, under the old system, was the Permanent Relief Employee, or PRE. A PRE was a permanent position whose job was to provide relief coverage — to fill in for absent route holders. PREs had permanent status, which meant they had more security than OCREs, but their actual work assignment could vary. They might cover different routes on different days, depending on who was absent. The PRE was the middle layer of the staffing structure: more stable than an OCRE, more flexible than a route holder.

The third classification is new: the Permanent Flex Employee, or PFE. Under the new agreement, all existing PREs are converted to PFEs. The name change reflects a real change in the job. A PFE is guaranteed a minimum of twenty scheduled hours per week — a floor that did not exist in the same way for PREs. They may be assigned to work up to forty hours. Their primary function is to cover vacation leaves and other absences. The twenty-hour guarantee is a genuine improvement: it gives converted PREs a predictable minimum income for the first time. But the flex in the name is doing real work. A PFE's schedule is, by design, more flexible and less predictable than a route holder's. They may be scheduled on different days, at different times, covering different routes. The guarantee of hours comes with the loss of a predictable routine.
The fourth classification is the OCRE — and this is where the new agreement makes its most significant changes to the staffing structure. Let us walk through what changes.

First, the geography. Under the old system, OCREs were organized into groups, and those groups were defined by geography. An OCRE group covered installations — post offices — within a fifteen-kilometre radius. When a route needed coverage, the OCRE would be called from within that local pool. The old system also had a restriction on filling vacant routes or Permanent Flex Employee positions: it was limited to OCREs who worked in installations located within a seventy-five-kilometre radius. That seventy-five-kilometre cap was a guardrail on how far an OCRE could be required to travel to fill a vacancy.

Both of those geographic parameters are changing. The OCRE group radius is expanding from fifteen kilometres to thirty kilometres. OCRE groups can now include co-located installations — multiple post offices treated as part of the same group — as long as they are managed by the same Local Area Manager. The Local Area Manager, often abbreviated as LAM, is the Canada Post supervisor responsible for a cluster of post offices in a region. So under the new rules, if several post offices in a region share a LAM, their OCREs can be grouped together, and an OCRE based at one office can be called to cover a route originating from another office within the expanded thirty-kilometre radius.

The seventy-five-kilometre restriction on filling vacant routes and PFE positions is being removed entirely. This means that when a route becomes vacant — when a route holder retires, or transfers, or the route is restructured — Canada Post can fill that vacancy with an OCRE from beyond the old seventy-five-kilometre limit. In principle, the removal of the geographic restriction gives management a much larger pool of OCREs to draw from when filling vacancies, which could mean that vacancies in hard-to-staff rural areas get filled more reliably. It could also mean that OCREs are asked to travel significant distances to cover routes far from their home office, with all the costs and disruptions that entails.

For a place like Port Hood, these geographic changes are not abstract. Port Hood is a small office in rural Cape Breton. The surrounding offices — Mabou, Judique, Cheticamp, Inverness — are also small. Under the old fifteen-kilometre OCRE group radius, an OCRE based at Port Hood might be called to cover routes in the immediate area but not much farther. Under the new thirty-kilometre radius, with co-located installations sharing a LAM, that same OCRE could be called to cover routes stretching along a much longer stretch of the western Cape Breton coast. The distance from Port Hood to Cheticamp is about seventy kilometres. The distance to Inverness is about forty. Whether those offices share a LAM, and whether the new grouping rules would pull an OCRE across that entire distance, depends on Canada Post's local management structure — details that are not spelled out in the collective agreement itself but will be determined as the new system is implemented.

There is a trade-off embedded in these changes, and it is the kind of trade-off that looks different depending on where you sit. From management's perspective, expanding the OCRE group radius and removing the vacancy restriction gives Canada Post the flexibility to ensure routes are covered. Rural offices have long struggled with coverage — when a route holder is absent and there is no OCRE available within the old radius, the route goes uncovered or has to be covered by someone working overtime, which is more expensive. A larger pool means more reliable coverage. From an OCRE's perspective, the changes mean being callable across a wider area, which could mean more work opportunities — more routes to cover, more hours available. But it could also mean being expected to drive significant distances to cover routes that are far from home, without necessarily being compensated for the additional travel time in the way a route holder would be. The flexibility that is a virtue from the employer's standpoint is, from the worker's standpoint, a deepening of the on-call, go-where-needed character of the job.

Second, the coverage hierarchy. The new agreement establishes a clearer set of rules for how uncovered routes get staffed — and this is the subject we will walk through in detail in the next chapter, because it is the most practical, operational change in the agreement. But the headline is this: for the first time, the collective agreement contains a structured framework for absence coverage, rather than leaving it largely to management's discretion. When a route is uncovered, there is now a defined order in which available workers are assigned. That order involves route holders, PFEs, and OCREs, and it is governed by seniority and ranking.

Third, and perhaps most consequentially for the most precarious workers: the People Plan memorandum, with its limited no-layoff commitment, does not cover OCREs. The commitment applies to permanent route holders and permanent flex employees only. On-Call Relief Employees, who are already the most precarious workers in the unit, receive no layoff protection under the transition plan. This is one of the sharpest edges of the agreement, and it is worth sitting with. The agreement protects the people who have permanent positions and leaves exposed the people who do not. If the workload measurement system recalculates routes downward, and fewer total hours of work are needed across an office, the adjustment will be absorbed by the classifications that can be reduced — and OCREs, with no guaranteed hours and no layoff protection, are the most absorbable.

CUPW's framing of these changes emphasizes the positive elements: the expanded OCRE group radius gives OCREs access to more work opportunities across a wider area; the removal of the vacancy restriction means OCREs can bid on and fill positions that were previously closed to them geographically; the new coverage hierarchy replaces management's unchecked discretion with a transparent, seniority-based system. All of this is true. The union also points out that the PRE-to-PFE conversion gives former relief workers a guaranteed minimum of twenty hours per week for the first time — a concrete improvement for a classification that previously had no such floor.

The critical framing emphasizes different facts: OCREs get no layoff protection, no guaranteed hours, and a wider geographic area they can be called across. The PFE's guaranteed twenty hours come with a more disrupted schedule. The removal of the seventy-five-kilometre restriction means OCREs can be drawn from much farther away, potentially displacing local workers from local vacancies. The new coverage hierarchy, while more transparent, is designed to maximize the use of the most flexible, least protected classifications.

Both framings, again, are honest descriptions of real features of the agreement. The question of whether the changes are, on balance, good for RSMCs depends on which classification you are in, how much you value predictability over opportunity, and whether you believe management will use the new flexibility to ensure coverage or to reduce costs. The answer will not be the same for every carrier, and it will not be the same in every office.

What the changes mean in operational terms — how an uncovered route actually gets staffed on a Tuesday morning when the regular carrier calls in sick — is the subject of the next chapter. Because it is there, in the concrete mechanics of absence coverage, that the new rules will be felt most directly by the people who work under them and the people who depend on the mail getting through.
Chapter 6 - Coverage of Absences

Picture a Tuesday morning in February. The regular carrier on Route 3 out of a rural post office — it does not matter which one, but let us say it is the kind of office that serves a coastal community and a stretch of inland rural boxes — has called in sick. Route 3 covers about ninety kilometres of road, serves a hundred and forty households, and includes three community mailbox clusters. It is a full day's work for someone who knows it. The mail has already been sorted at the plant and trucked to the office overnight. Parcels are waiting on the dock. The route needs to go out, and the person who usually runs it is not here.

Under the old collective agreement, what happened next was governed more by custom and management discretion than by clear contractual rules. The agreement said very little about how absences were to be covered, apart from vacation leave. Management had broad authority to assign employees to uncovered routes, and the criteria for who got assigned, in what order, and from how far away were often unclear, inconsistent from office to office, and a source of friction. Carriers would file grievances — formal complaints under the collective agreement — challenging assignments they believed were made unfairly or in violation of understood seniority rights. The system worked, in the sense that routes generally got covered, but it worked through a combination of informal practices, local arrangements, and the assumption that someone would always be found to take the route, often on short notice, often an OCRE who had little choice but to accept.

For many years, CUPW went to the bargaining table demanding that Canada Post agree to clear, contractual rules for absence coverage. The union's argument was that leaving coverage to management discretion created a system that was unpredictable for workers, inconsistent across offices, and structurally biased toward using the most flexible classifications rather than giving permanent employees predictable access to additional work. Canada Post's argument was that rigid coverage rules would reduce the corporation's ability to ensure routes were covered efficiently, especially in smaller offices with limited staffing.

In the new agreement, CUPW got what it had been asking for: a structured framework for coverage of absences, written into the contract. Whether this framework represents a win for workers, a win for management, or something more complicated depends on the details, and the details are worth walking through carefully.

Here is how the new coverage system works, in principle. When a route is uncovered — because the regular carrier is absent — the route is first offered to route holders in the office. A route holder can choose to bid on the uncovered route, meaning they volunteer to cover it, typically in addition to or instead of their own route. This is the first tier of the hierarchy: permanent employees with assigned routes get the first opportunity to pick up additional work. If a route holder bids on the uncovered route, they cover it, and their own route — or the portion of the work they are not doing because they are covering the absent carrier — becomes the new absence that needs to be filled.

That resulting absence — the gap created when a route holder shifts to cover another route — is then filled by a PFE or an OCRE. This is the second tier. Between the PFE and the OCRE, assignment is based on seniority or ranking order. A PFE, as a permanent employee with the twenty-hour guarantee, generally has priority over an OCRE, who is not permanent and has no guaranteed hours. Within each classification, the worker with greater seniority — or, for OCREs, the earlier ranking date — gets the assignment first.

So the hierarchy, in plain terms, is: first, route holders who volunteer; second, the PFE by seniority; third, the OCRE by ranking date. This is a real change from the old system, where the order was often unclear and management could assign coverage based on operational convenience rather than a contractual sequence.

There is a concrete logic to this hierarchy, and it reflects a genuine tension in how absence coverage works. From the route holder's perspective, having the first opportunity to bid on uncovered routes is valuable: it gives permanent employees a chance to earn additional income, to cover routes they might eventually want to bid on permanently, and to have control over their work rather than having coverage imposed on them. From the PFE's perspective, being second in the hierarchy means reliable access to the work that is their primary function — covering absences. From the OCRE's perspective, being third means you get called when no one above you in the hierarchy is available, which is both the reality of being the most flexible classification and the reason the job is so unpredictable.

Now let us walk through that Tuesday morning again, under the new rules. The route holder on Route 3 has called in sick. The supervisor — or in rural offices, the Local Area Manager — checks whether any route holders in the office want to bid on covering Route 3. One route holder, who finishes her own route early most days, volunteers. She will cover Route 3 today, and the portion of her own route she will not get to becomes the new uncovered work. A PFE is available and, by seniority, is assigned to cover the remaining work on the volunteer's route. The PFE gets a full day's work, the route holder earns additional pay, and Route 3 goes out on time.

That is the smooth version. Here is a harder version. The route holder on Route 3 calls in sick. No route holder in the office volunteers to cover it — it is a long route, the weather is bad, and everyone has their own work. The PFE who would normally cover is already assigned elsewhere. So the LAM calls an OCRE. Under the new rules, the OCRE called is the one with the earliest ranking date among available OCREs in the group — and the group, remember, is now a thirty-kilometre radius, potentially including co-located offices. The OCRE drives to the office, loads the vehicle, and runs Route 3. The OCRE gets a day's work. Route 3 goes out. But the OCRE may have driven thirty kilometres to get to the office, will drive ninety kilometres running the route, and may not be compensated for the additional travel in the same way a route holder would be. The work got done. The cost of doing it fell, in part, on the worker in the most precarious position.

This is the tension that lives inside the new coverage framework. It is more transparent than what came before, and transparency is genuinely valuable — carriers can now point to a contractual hierarchy rather than relying on informal practices or filing grievances. But the hierarchy is designed to channel coverage work through the system in a way that maximizes the use of available labor at the lowest cost. Route holders bidding first means the corporation does not have to pay a relief worker. PFEs second means the twenty-hour guarantee is used productively. OCREs third means the most flexible, least compensated workers fill the remaining gaps. Each step in the hierarchy serves both the worker in that classification and the employer's interest in efficient coverage. The framework does not resolve the tension between those interests. It structures it.

There is another dimension to the coverage rules that matters for rural offices specifically, and it has to do with the relationship between the coverage hierarchy and the geographic changes we discussed in the last chapter. The expanded OCRE group radius — fifteen to thirty kilometres, with co-located installations — and the removal of the seventy-five-kilometre vacancy restriction together mean that the pool of relief workers available to cover a rural route is significantly larger than it was. In one sense, this is good for coverage: a rural office that previously had one or two local OCREs can now draw on OCREs from a wider area, reducing the chance that a route goes uncovered. In another sense, it changes the character of relief work in rural communities. If the OCRE covering Route 3 used to be someone from the same community — a neighbor, a person whose kids went to the same school — and is now someone called from thirty or fifty kilometres away, the work has been done, but something about the local fabric of the postal service has shifted.

Canada Post would argue, not unreasonably, that the priority is ensuring the mail goes out, and that a larger pool of relief workers serves that priority. CUPW would argue, also not unreasonably, that the expanded radius and removed restrictions give management tools to centralize and standardize coverage in ways that erode the local, community-based character of rural delivery. Both are describing real dynamics, and both are partially right.

What the new coverage framework does, more than anything else, is replace a system of discretion with a system of rules. Whether that is an improvement depends on what you thought of the discretion. For workers who experienced the old system as arbitrary — subject to favoritism, inconsistency, or management's operational priorities — the new rules are a gain. For workers who benefited from local arrangements that the new rules override, or who worry that the rules are designed to optimize for cost rather than for workers' interests, the new framework is a more complicated proposition.

There is a dimension of the coverage rules that operates above the level of individual absences, and it concerns the relationship between coverage, staffing levels, and the new classifications. The coverage hierarchy works — it produces reliable route coverage — only if there are enough people in the hierarchy to draw on. If an office has route holders who do not want to bid on additional routes, no available PFE, and no OCRE in the group, the hierarchy produces no coverage. The route goes uncovered, and the mail does not go out. This is the operational reality that the geographic expansions — the thirty-kilometre OCRE radius, the removal of the vacancy restriction — are designed to address: by enlarging the pool, the system increases the probability that someone is available. But it also means that the coverage hierarchy is only as strong as the staffing. And staffing is where the structural tension between Canada Post's cost-containment logic and CUPW's job-security logic plays out most directly. A leaner workforce — fewer PFEs, fewer OCREs per office — is cheaper, but it means the coverage hierarchy is thinner, and absences are harder to absorb. A larger workforce provides better coverage but costs more. The collective agreement does not fully resolve this tension. It structures the hierarchy and leaves the staffing levels to be determined through the operational and budget decisions that will follow.

What is unambiguous is that the framework will be tested in the coming years, in thousands of individual coverage decisions, in offices across the country. How route holders, PFEs, and OCREs experience the hierarchy in practice — whether seniority is respected, whether OCREs are called fairly, whether the geographic expansion delivers better coverage or just more travel for precarious workers — will determine whether the new rules are remembered as an improvement or as a more efficient version of the same precarity.
Chapter 7 - Weekend Parcels and the Amazon Question

Canada Post delivers letters Monday through Friday. It has for as long as most Canadians can remember. But the new collective agreement introduces something different: a formal framework for weekend parcel delivery. This is not a minor scheduling adjustment. It is the leading edge of a strategic shift in what Canada Post is for, who it competes with, and what kind of workforce it employs. And it is one of the features of the agreement that both sides — CUPW and Canada Post — describe in very different terms, while pointing at exactly the same set of facts.

To understand why weekend parcels are in the contract, you have to understand what has happened to the postal business over the last fifteen years. Letter mail — the thing Canada Post was built to carry — has been in steady decline. Email replaced personal letters. Online billing replaced paper statements. Digital marketing replaced bulk mail. The volume of transaction mail — bills, statements, government correspondence — that flows through the system is a fraction of what it was a decade ago, and it continues to fall. Canada Post's own financial reporting attributes part of its recent revenue decline to this structural shift, noting that even direct marketing is shrinking as advertisers move to digital channels.

Parcels are the opposite story. The rise of e-commerce — Amazon, online retailers, meal kits, subscription boxes, and everything else that arrives in a cardboard box on a doorstep — has made parcel delivery the growth area in logistics. More Canadians are buying more things online, and those things need to be delivered. Canada Post, with its national network, its fleet, and its workforce, is positioned to carry those parcels. But it is not the only option, and in many segments it is not the leading option. Amazon operates its own logistics network. FedEx, UPS, and DHL compete in the express segment. Purolator — which is owned by Canada Post but operates as a separate company — competes in the commercial parcel market. Regional carriers and gig-economy delivery services handle last-mile delivery in major cities.

What Canada Post saw, and what the federal government saw, was a Crown corporation whose traditional revenue base was eroding while the growth opportunity — parcels — was being captured by competitors. The corporation was losing money, and the losses were accelerating. Something had to change, and one of the changes was to compete more aggressively for parcel business by offering what the market increasingly expects: weekend delivery. When someone orders something online on a Friday and sees a promised delivery date of Saturday or Sunday, they expect a carrier that operates seven days a week. Canada Post, operating five days a week for letter mail, was structurally disadvantaged in the parcel market it most needed to win.

This is the logic behind the weekend parcel delivery model in the new collective agreement. The agreement establishes a framework for weekend operations — specifically, a new classification of worker called a Parcel Delivery Part-Time employee, or PD PT, assigned to centralized parcel delivery installations. These are not the local post offices that serve communities like Port Hood. They are larger, centralized facilities — delivery depots designed to process and dispatch parcels efficiently across a wider area. PD PT employees would primarily be scheduled on weekends, when the volume of parcels to be delivered is high and the regular weekday workforce is off.

How many PD PT employees are needed, and how are their hours determined? The answer is in the agreement, and it reveals the management philosophy driving the model. Staffing levels for the parcel delivery operation are determined through formulas that divide parcel volume by activity-per-hour productivity targets. In plain language: management calculates how many parcels need to be delivered, divides that by the number of parcels each worker is expected to deliver per hour, and arrives at the number of worker-hours required. If productivity targets increase — if the expected parcels-per-hour goes up — fewer workers are needed for the same volume. If volume drops, fewer workers are needed at the same productivity target. The workforce scales up and down with the formula.

This is worth pausing on, because it is a fundamentally different model from how traditional postal work has been organized. A route holder has a route, and the route is their work, and they do it every day regardless of whether it is a heavy day or a light one. A PD PT employee does not have a route in that sense. They have an assignment determined by the formula, and the assignment exists only when the formula says it is needed. This is the logic of a parcel logistics operation — the same logic that companies like Amazon use to manage their delivery workforce. It allows the operation to scale with demand, minimize idle labor costs, and maximize the output per worker-hour.

There is a tension here that the agreement does not resolve, because it cannot. The weekend parcel model is, on one reading, a reasonable response to a competitive reality. Canada Post needs to compete for parcel business to survive, and competing means offering the service levels that customers expect. Without weekend delivery, the corporation loses parcel volume to competitors, and losing parcel volume accelerates the financial crisis that is already threatening the institution. The PD PT classification creates the workforce to support that competitive position. It is a survival strategy.

On another reading, the weekend parcel model is the mechanism for transforming Canada Post from a public service into a parcel logistics company. The shift from full-time route holders to formula-driven part-time workers is the shift from a stable, career workforce to a flexible, precarious one. The PD PT classification, with its weekend-focused scheduling and formula-determined hours, is a template for how new postal jobs will be structured: scaled to demand, minimized in cost, maximized in productivity. The parcel economy does not need career postal workers who know their routes and their communities. It needs workers who can deliver a target number of parcels per hour, on the days the formula says they are needed, and who do not cost very much when the formula says they are not.

Both of these readings are present in the public discussion of the agreement, and both are honestly held. Canada Post's framing — that weekend delivery is necessary to rebuild the business and restore confidence in the postal service — is a genuine description of the competitive pressure the corporation faces. CUPW's framing — that the agreement creates new classifications that erode stable, full-time work — is a genuine description of what the PD PT classification represents in labor terms. The critical framing — that this is the Amazonification of Canada Post, a turn toward a precarious workforce organized around parcel productivity targets — is a genuine description of the structural parallel with private logistics companies.

The difference between these framings is not about the facts. Everyone agrees on what the agreement says. The difference is about what you think the facts mean, and that depends on what you believe Canada Post is for. If Canada Post is primarily a business that needs to compete in a logistics market, then the weekend parcel model is a necessary adaptation. If Canada Post is primarily a public service whose value includes stable employment and universal coverage, then the model is a concession that trades those values for competitiveness. The agreement does not choose between these visions. It creates the apparatus for both, and leaves the resolution to the coming years of implementation.

There is a question that rural communities should be asking about all of this, and it is not the question that dominates the public discussion. The weekend parcel model is built around centralized parcel delivery installations serving areas of high parcel density — that is, urban and suburban areas where the volume justifies the operation. The formula-driven staffing model works where there are enough parcels per hour to make the calculation meaningful. A rural route along the Cape Breton coast, with a hundred and forty households spread over ninety kilometres, does not generate the parcel density that a suburban subdivision in Ottawa generates. The weekend parcel model, as described in the agreement, is an urban and suburban phenomenon.

What this means for rural communities is an open question, and one that the agreement does not fully answer. If Canada Post's strategic future is parcel delivery, and parcel delivery is concentrated in urban and suburban areas, then the investment, the new classifications, and the operational focus will flow toward those areas. The rural routes — served by RSMCs, paid under the new hourly system, staffed through the coverage hierarchy we walked through in the last chapter — exist in a different strategic reality. They are not the growth area. They are, in business terms, the legacy service. The question is whether a Crown corporation under financial pressure, with a government mandate to transform, will sustain investment in a legacy service that does not generate the revenue that the new model is built to capture.

Canada Post has said, publicly, that it remains committed to protecting postal services in rural, remote, and Indigenous communities throughout the transformation process. That commitment is stated, and it matters. But stated commitments and budgetary realities do not always align, and the structural logic of the parcel-focused transformation — investment where the revenue is, scaling where the volume supports it — does not naturally flow toward low-density rural areas. This is one of the things worth watching as the agreement is implemented: not just what the contract says about rural service, but where the actual investment goes, where the new classifications are deployed, and whether rural routes are maintained, consolidated, or quietly allowed to erode.

The weekend parcel model, in other words, is not just about weekend parcels. It is a window into the strategic direction of Canada Post, and into the question of whether that direction has room for the kind of postal service that rural communities have historically relied on. The answer is not in the contract. It is in the years ahead.
Chapter 8 - The Money Problem

The collective agreement we have been examining does not exist in a vacuum. It exists inside a financial emergency, and understanding that emergency is the only way to understand why the agreement looks the way it does. The wage increases, the structural changes, the new classifications, the weekend parcel model — all of them are responses, at different levels, to a single underlying condition: Canada Post is losing money at a rate that threatens its viability as currently structured, and the federal government has decided that the response to that condition is transformation, not subsidy.

Let us start with the numbers, because they are stark. In the first quarter of 2026 — the period from January through March — Canada Post reported a loss before tax of two hundred and five million dollars. That is not an annual figure. That is three months. The loss represented a deterioration of one hundred and sixty-four million dollars compared to the same period in 2025, when the corporation lost forty-one million. Revenue fell by one hundred and eighty-one million dollars, or fourteen point three percent, year over year. Every major revenue category declined. Parcel revenue dropped seventy-nine million dollars — a seventeen percent fall — because customers, shaken by the prolonged labour uncertainty and the 2024 strike, shifted their deliveries to competitors offering more certainty. Transaction mail revenue fell eighty-two million dollars. Direct marketing revenue declined twenty-four million. The corporation warned, in its own quarterly results, that parcel volumes would be slow to win back, reinforcing what it called the critical need to transform in a competitive market.

At the group level — the Canada Post Group of Companies, which includes Purolator — the loss was even larger: two hundred and fifty-one million dollars before tax in the first quarter.
